sentences.text: I don't know.
I think it was so typical.
It's not like nowadays.
Kids have plans and dreams.
And you just existed.
You went to school.
You ate.
You slept.
I guess the dreams were-- you just couldn't afford to dream, you know?
You couldn't afford to dream.
OK.
I wanted-- I-- I wanted-- actually, a dream-- I wanted to play the piano.
It was like so exciting.
But then again, that wasn't very-- very possible.
It wasn't available.
You needed a piano.
And so we substituted.
Like, my sister played the guitar.
And we would sing.
We used to sing a lot, you know?
My father would join in.
And it was even comical.
Like, Hanukkah, we used to play games.
And we would play-- it's called [NON-ENGLISH], which is Bingo.
And then we call out number-- B71, or whatever, you know?
And then, in sleep, my father would-- oh, yeah, I was talking about singing.
Sometimes he would sing out at night.
Or also with that Bingo, I started saying, he would call in his sleep, I 15.
[LAUGHS] And I didn't need any more.
I was teasing him.
But as far as singing, there was a lot of singing.
Oh, everything was favorite-- Polish songs and, later on, the Russians.
They so sentimental.
Oh, my God!
How do you know I can sing?
[LAUGHS] Or even Yiddish songs.
Like-- [SINGING IN YIDDISH] And so on.
[LAUGHS] And Polish songs, they were every-- you know, every popular song.
The hit parades-- boy, did I know all of them-- although I was the youngest.
But I had two older sisters.
And I knew every-- every song that was popular.
And we had a radio, which was not such a-- that was the time when radios weren't a common thing.
But we had telephone [NON-ENGLISH], telephone [NON-ENGLISH].
And-- and I had to listen to-- I-- not I had to.
But I was glued to that radio, picking up all the songs.
And next day in school, I had to tell all the kids what songs are popular, because you didn't have a radio to-- to-- [LAUGHS]
When I was young?
Well, some from school.
But mostly, you would have friends who-- in the neighborhood, because it wasn't easy to, you know, to-- to go visit somewhere far.
So I had so, so many cousins who were my-- approximately my age.
And we would always play, always.
We were always outdoors.
There wasn't much to do indoors.
In summer, we spent our days-- on-- during vacation time, we had a beautiful river in our town.
Oh, that river was-- this is where we existed all summer.
It was beautiful, beautiful.
And in wintertime-- outdoors-- tobogganing, skating.
I would go.
I only did it with pleasure.
My mother would send me on an errand.
On my skates I skated down, no matter how far-- always outdoors, yeah.
Well, I-- as life progresses, you know, my-- my best friend was my sister.
When I was young, I used to drag along, you know, always running after her.
And then I had-- I had friends which, believe it or not, I don't even remember-- Rivkah was her name.
I don't even remember her family name.
I just-- it just-- time--
--time erases your memory.
Oh, no, no, no.
Oh, no-- did not, no.
We-- we lived like so-- so separated-- far away.
It would not have been possible.
No, no, there were no-- Jews lived separately.
My oldest sister, she would-- she was in that [NON-ENGLISH] Gymnasium, which was actually mainly non-Jewish people.
Only a few select-- it was like a feather in your cap.
If you were able to go to that gymnasium-- and-- which was, in the last years, like before the war, with Hitler's influence, all the students were already terribly anti-Semitic.
And they wore little pins behind their lapel.
And they would display it like this.
Aha, you see?
I belong to the Hitler Organization.
And it became simply impossible to exist there any longer.
They were-- they were just tearing the few Jewish kids apart.
So she had to switch to the other gymnasium, which was like a-- only Jewish people.
Oh, always.
Our school was-- was separated.
First of all, our schools were not co-ed.
Girls-- Jewish girls separate, Jewish boys separate; Christian boys separate, Christian girls separate.
Our-- our school, the school I-- I went to, right next to it-- just dividing-- a fence was dividing the two schools-- were the boys' Christian school.
We were spat at.
We were thrown stones, and always names, always names and laughing, you know, for no reason.
But it-- it-- it didn't even cross our mind to go to report to the principal.
Hey, he threw a stone at me.
I mean, how dare you?
You are Jewish, so you expect it.
No, you have no idea how it became-- it-- it was like, you have no rights.
It-- This was even before Hitler's rise to power?
Yeah!
In Poland, a child would be born with that instinct.
It's like later on, if I happened to recall it-- to relate-- when we were running, my little friend and I, from one place, we couldn't hide here.
We ran to somebody else.
During the day, luckily, the Nazis, the German, did not find us.
If they find us, we would be finished right there.
But there were two little kids, maybe five and six.
And they started calling, [NON-ENGLISH].
It's like a-- a-- a name, a derogatory-- Jewish names-- [NON-ENGLISH].
How?
I was saying, how does a little kid-- and we run.
It was winter.
And everything was covered.
Like, hardly you could see.
But it seemed to me that a-- a-- a Polish-- or a Ukrainian-- even worse-- but we were not so much-- like, next door, more to the Poles than the Ukrainian.
It's like they're born with the instinct that you're a Jew.
And if you're a Jew, you can't do anything.
They were throwing stones at us, laughing, and--
No, no, not personally.
I mean, you knew to stay away, to stay out of the way.
Don't-- but it-- what-- what bothers me now, knowing-- like, being now in a modern and a different world-- not even-- it didn't even cross your mind to go in and complain.
Hey, they-- they bother us.
They throw stones.
They spit at us.
No way.
Oh, you-- don't you say anything.
No, no, it was-- it was a matter of fact.
It as a matter of fact.
I mean, this was-- anti-Semitism, it wasn't something-- oh, my God, what's happening?
You were prepared for it.
Well, in '33 or '34--
--maybe-- I wouldn't know.
I don't know.
Maybe my parents knew.
But I-- I had better things to do than follow politics.
But as it was nearing the war, I would say in 1938, '39, of course-- probably '38 mostly-- as I say, we had a radio.
And 1/2 of the neighborhood, or maybe the whole neighborhood, would gather under the-- under the window, you know.
The window was open.
Everybody would listen to his speeches.
And it was terribly scary.
All you heard is roaring-- and the Juden!
The Juden!
All we-- all you-- because you didn't understand really much of the German.
Although it's similar to Jewish, so you could pick up.
But all you heard is the Juden, the Juden, which means the Jews, the Jews.
That was the-- and Poland became, as I say, like the boys in-- in that gymnasium.
They already were proud to belong to the Hitler group and had a little pin underneath there to show that they belong.
And it became-- I'll tell you an anti-Semitic-- if I can.
As I say, my sister was in that gymnasium.
My mother-- my parents were very proud, because that really was prestige.
And it was Christmastime, and they had a party.
And they were exchanging gifts.
And they had a fir tree on the stage.
I was then very young.
I don't remember-- but young, not like a pre-teen or a teenager.
I was a kid.
My mother took me with-- with them.
And they exchanged gifts.
Then come to the Jewish girls.
They gave a gift to a Jewish girl-- a big box.
They open up the box.
And there-- another box.
Then they opened the other box-- and a carton, actually-- another one.
And then in the small one-- an onion.
It-- I mean, then it didn't hurt me.
You could spit in my face.
You-- what-- what else do I expect?
By when I think back now, if you don't give me a gift-- fine, you know.
You don't like me-- fine.
But to take-- to take the effort to really mock you, after all these boxes-- and this is on the stage, where everybody called out, now for this girl, an onion.
So this explains.
Yeah.
First of all, we were always in danger.
Anti-Semitism-- that's why we lived very much apart, because otherwise, there would have been conflict every day, all day.
What happened is then they started-- you would hear the radio.
Or you felt it.
Of course, even if you didn't have a radio, you-- you felt-- Jew, Jew, Jew.
Jew, go to Palestine.
That was a motto.
Jew go to Palestine.
That was something that you heard since.
They started talking, the Poles, about-- not the Poles like the neighbors, but the authorities-- about-- it was Hitler's influence-- to take all the Jews and send them to Madagascar.
I mean, we knew.
We-- we learned geography.
Here it's not compulsory.
But for us, everything was compulsory-- history, geography, everything.
But Madagascar was such a-- an exotic-- I don't know.
It's such a strange, fun place.
We didn't travel very far, you know, to-- to the-- like, nowadays, the world is small.
You know the whole world.
But then-- so-- but we knew that it's Africa.
And it's-- it's wild.
It's savage.
So they wanted to take-- this was the plan circulated, that they would-- will gather up all the Jews and send them on the-- on the-- on Madagascar-- to Madagascar.
There were even songs that describe-- that says-- I don't want to sing it.
But Madagascar, the wild country, the savage country.
But maybe there it will be better, because where there is culture, there-- it's no good.
Where there is culture, there is-- there is-- there were-- there is culture.
There is persecution.
There is-- there is a dues there, you know?
Like this idea-- maybe it's better where there is wilderness.
Did your family make any attempts to leave the country?
Oh, no, no, no-- nobody, nobody, nobody.
This was your place.
You didn't know any other place.
There was no place to leave-- to go where?
The youth, that was very-- very prevalent.
Youth were going to Palestine, not in masses.
But you know, this patriotism stirred up.
The more we were persecuted, the more-- the more patriotism.
There were these organizations-- Betar and-- and Shomer Hatzair, you know?
And they instigated that all.
We have to, like-- let's-- we-- we couldn't fight.
Nobody was fighting.
There was no such a thing-- a Jew would fight back.
He took anything that-- that you-- you did to him.
But we must go to our land, to Palestine, and build our land.
And this is the only place where-- that we dreamed of going to.
That was the only place that maybe will accept us.
I was young.
I belonged for a while, because the boys were there, you know, like-- but not really out of conviction, or-- no, not-- these were older boys, you know, somebody who would leave home already.
And quite a few left for Palestine, you know.
I-- gee, I-- I was wondering if it was Akiba.
Akiba?
You see, I don't even-- for a short while, only-- as I say, not because of my convictions.
I believed I--
No.
Not that-- no-- I don't think anybody.
You had no connections.
You-- the world was-- this is-- this was your world.
You didn't know any world outside.
Maybe they buried some jewelry.
I don't know.
Maybe they buried some silverware, but-- but-- but not-- not yet.
But when we already saw black and white that the end is coming, you know, that--
Oh, black and white-- first, when--
No.
When-- when the Germans attacked again, in 1941--
OK, yeah.
Of war, it was in the air.
We knew politics.
You-- you listen to the radio.
You-- you-- in our home, they read the the Jewish paper.
The paper wrote that Hitler is arising.
And the anti-Semitism is even bigger, much, much greater design than-- than we ever knew.
So we knew.
But I don't know why a Jew was so meek.
So you have to sit and wait for the end.
There's no escape.
[DRINKS] So what happened?
[DRINKS]
And then the war started in 1939.
And Hitler attacks.
And for three days, actually, my hometown-- the bombs fell.
My best friend-- as I say, I couldn't remember her-- Bichler, I think.
She, her sister, and her father were-- were killed when the first-- the first bomb fell.
And the city was devastated.
It was a lot of glass, I remember, and broken telephone-- not so much telephone.
Telephone was a rarity-- power lines, you know?
And it lasted three-- and the Germans drove into town, you know.
I remember these young boys-- blond, healthy-- on motorcycles.
We still did not know.
Oh, no, it can't be this bad.
You know, you still have high hopes.
And-- but that lasted three days.
And then we heard that Russia and Germany made a pact, and they divide Poland in 1/2-- the eastern part Russia grabs, and the western Germany.
And where I lived-- that was the Ukraine-- it went to the Russian side.
So there, as far as I was, you know, a young girl, for me-- first of all, it was very exciting when the Russians came in with the tanks.
And, you know, everybody-- and all the kids ran too.
It was so-- but when do you see tanks rolling on your little streets, you know, and the Russians waving?
It was euphoria.
So to me, it was-- ah, it was so exciting, terrific.
And then the Russians came, of course, and introduced their system, which means liquidating every private business, which my father's business was taken away.
There was no such a thing like you're a proprietor of something.
But we had-- in that committee that was-- how do you-- how do you say?
Confiscating-- was a friend of ours-- and he gave us notice that, let's say, Thursday, they're going to confiscate my father's business.
So we would make a few trips and salvage a little.
There were boxes of powder.
There were perfumed soap.
You know, in those days, most people didn't use fancy aromatic soap.
You know, whatever is-- it was good enough that they washed, you know?
But we had soaps.
It was like a-- a jewel, you know?
So we, whatever we could, we brought home, which later on came in very handy.
When we were in the ghetto and had nothing else to eat, the Pole would sneak up to the fence.
We would give him a bar of soap.
He would give us two potatoes or something.
So that came in so handy.
So your father was able to salvage many things, or--
No, a little.
We just took out whatever, figuring maybe-- not that we needed the powder to powder ourselves.
But maybe it will be-- you need it.
So the Russians came in.
And then everything went peacefully.
It-- I mean, except-- for me-- probably, my father didn't like it-- when you take away a business that you built all your life and this is your livelihood, you know?
But to me, Russian school was terrific, because Russians, they had holidays, parties, dancing.
Every so often there was-- and I was-- we were four girls and four boys-- dancers.
And they were always-- in Russia, you know, this is the way they kept the people happy.
There's always music, the accordion, and you dance.
And you sing.
You know, it was a-- [LAUGHS] --and so I would dance away my-- I-- I loved it, the two years in Russian school.
But then, after two years-- and then, you know, I don't know.
This is like a political thing.
I don't know.
I don't know how much I can talk.
[LAUGHS]
I could talk for days and never end.
You see, when-- when the Russians occupied this part, the Germans-- the western part, all the Jews-- I believe that all the Jews came running to the east.
They were afraid to stay with the Germans.
So it became so congested.
There was no place to cope.
There was no place-- impossible to cope with such a volume of-- of people.
So the Russians sent them to Siberia, those-- the refugees.
We stayed in our home.
But the refugees were all sent to Siberia, which a lot-- a lot of my friends-- that became friends later.
And actually, if you see, from Polish people who survived with a mother, a father, an uncle-- because we-- it was very seldom that you survive with a sibling.
But those in Siberia, they had a very hard time there.
But they weren't killed-- unless somebody died.
So these were the people who, you see-- like, I've had, later on, after the war, friends.
And they had a mother and a sister, you know, because they were in Siberia.
Because the-- this-- the Russians didn't know what to do with so many people.
And then Siberia, they come in handy.
They can work there.
Yeah, so the Russian years were, as far as I'm concerned-- I was concerned, was good.
Actually, if anybody was anti-Semitic, it was within her soul.
No, actually not, because it was forbidden.
It was against the constitution.
Everybody is equal.
Of course, now everything comes out, you know.
Because-- but no, there was no-- they were a little-- I almost used a bad word.
They were turned off about Jews, because they said that the Jew is trying to get out of the front line.
You know, he's saying he's sick or whatever, you know?
A Jew didn't want to go to war.
[LAUGHS]
Especially the-- well, the first world war, you know, they even cut off a toe or whatever, so I was told-- just anything.
Because how can he survive?
It has to be kosher.
And how do you survive in an army with-- so anyway.
So there was less anti-Semitism?
Is that what--
Oh, no.
I would say--
--to my knowledge, actually none.
Actually none.
No, rock-- no, rock throwing, that was Polish.
That was Polish.
That was before '39.
'39 To '41, you did not really-- no, you did not really feel any.
Maybe it was-- no, you did not.
Oh, Polish.
Oh, no, the Poles were subdued.
You know, the Poles were-- were taken.
They didn't like too much the Poles either, because Poles were very patriotic-- my Poland.
That's true Very-- because they had to be-- being situated geographically between the two enemies who were always tearing Poland apart, they became very self-centered-- and, we Poles.
But the Russians-- no, no, no-- a Pole wouldn't dare throwing a stone then, no.
It was--
After, they sent him-- because everybody had to do something.
In Russia, you didn't sit, and-- or your father would take care of you, you know?
Like, let's say a student-- there was no-- everybody had to do their part.
And he was sent to a neighboring city, which was called Ustyluh, to work in the pharmacy there.
And this is-- and he used to come in on the weekends.
So he worked in Ustyluh.
And this was like until '41.
And as I say, in 1941, when Germans attack again-- because now they have-- they so close.
Now we are Russia.
And they are just next door to Russia.
And this was their aim, to capture Russia, especially starting with the Ukraine, the bread basket, the oil, I mean, all the riches, the natural resources.
And so the war starts.
And my father is still there.
And immediately, like, we-- we hear the heavy artillery, because we were just-- we were the beginning of the whole course-- and the artillery.
And I remember my mother being frantic that my father is away.
But he showed up the next day, I think, walking.
I don't know how.
Because there wasn't like, I'll take a train.
I'll take a bus.
I'll take a car, or whatever.
And in any case, he showed up and came back.
And this was the beginning already of the tragic end-- the beginning, very beginning.
Hitler attacks.
They come in.
Immediately, they set up posts to register, of course, very important data for the future plans.
How many there are, how many women, how many men, how many children, ages, everything, so they know exactly what they are dealing with, you know.
No, no, no, everyone.
I remember we went.
Like, I went with my friends, and-- and they would even joke-- the Germans.
They set up, like, in schools.
They set up posts.
Everybody had to register.
And we were very obedient people.
Because God forbid-- I mean, we always afraid.
You have to do what the government, the authorities say-- very afraid of authorities, policemen, anybody authority.
And I went with my little friends.
And they were joking.
I don't know.
We were so young.
They said, married?
And, oh, we giggled-- so embarrassed-- married, you know.
So of course, they had to ask a question, because they had to know exactly where, what, where we live, how old we are, and what sex we are of.
So it was registered now.
They know everything.
And they immediately started gathering up, relocating people.
No, first they started building barbed wire, you know, enclosures; and relocating people who happened to be-- they-- they created two ghettos.
And they-- they relocated people who, let's say, didn't live-- lived in between everybody.
Like, in our house-- I don't know.
We had five rooms or something.
And in each room, there was a family, because they needed you compact, you know, in one place.
And they even brought the people, the Jews from the neighboring little towns or villages.
Everybody-- very smart, very well-organized-- this you have to grant the Germans-- organized.
Everything was now cooped up.
So now--
--they have-- you see, we didn't have concentration camps.
They weren't-- the bright idea didn't come up yet.
But we had-- we were cooped up behind barbed wire.
And they created two ghettos.
I don't know.
But each room had a family.
I didn't even know.
